coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of a multicomponent catalytic system Bi2O3/Bi2S3/CDs with an exceptional catalytic activity in the photocatalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Jun
Ji, Rui
Gu, Pei-Yang
Wang, Peng
Zhou, Shiyuan
Chen, Zhidong
Xu, Qingfeng
coredata.dc:description: Industrial and agricultural wastewater containing 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has serious toxic effects on human beings and environments. We reported a novel visible-light-driven nanocomposite with carbon dots (CDs) doped on the surface of Bi2O3/Bi2S3 (abbreviated as BB), that is BB/CDs, acted as an efficient photocatalyst for 4-NP reduction. The structure, morphology and optical properties of the BB/CDs composite were characterized and investigated in detail. The reduction from 4-NP to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) could be completed within only 2 min over the optimized composite in the presence of NaBH4, while for saturated 4-NP solution, the completed reduction is within 1 h. This simple catalytic system might provide a new method for both industrial application and environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bi2O3/Bi2S3
Photocatalytic reduction
4-nitrophenol
4-aminophenol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) BB and (b) BB/CDs-8; (c-d) TEM images of BB/CDs-8 composite. (e) STEM and (f)-(i) the corresponding elemental mapping images of BB/CDs-8 composite.
(a)The XRD patterns of pure BB, CDs and BB/CDs-8 composite; (b)The Raman spectra of BB, CDs and BB/CDs-X composites with different contents of CDs.
The XPS analysis of the BB/CDs-8 composite: (a) the full XPS spectrum, (b) the C 1 s, (c)Bi 4f and (d) S 2 s high-resolution XPS spectra.

(a) Photocatalytic activity and (b) plots of ln(C0/Ct) of as-prepared CDs, BB, BB/CDs-8 for 4-NP reduction (500 ppm) under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). (c) Photocatalytic activity and (d) plots of ln(C0/Ct) of as-prepared BB, BB/CDs-8 for 4-NP reduction (saturated solution) under visible-light irradiation or dark condition.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure to obtain BB/CDs composite.
Possible mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the BB/CDs composite system.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of a multicomponent catalytic system Bi2O3/Bi2S3/CDs with an exceptional catalytic activity in the photocatalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial and agricultural wastewater containing 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) has serious toxic effects on human beings and environments. We reported a novel visible-light-driven nanocomposite with carbon dots (CDs) doped on the surface of Bi2O3/Bi2S3 (abbreviated as BB), that is BB/CDs, acted as an efficient photocatalyst for 4-NP reduction. The structure, morphology and optical properties of the BB/CDs composite were characterized and investigated in detail. The reduction from 4-NP to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) could be completed within only 2 min over the optimized composite in the presence of NaBH4, while for saturated 4-NP solution, the completed reduction is within 1 h. This simple catalytic system might provide a new method for both industrial application and environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: P-nitrophenol (4-NP), as a typical aromatic nitro compound and an important intermediate in the chemical and pharmaceutical industry, is one of the most common pollutants derived from the industrial wastewater and difficult to be degraded in aqueous solutions, leading to significant damage to the central nervous system, liver, kidneys and blood of humans [1–3]. Therefore, the effective removal of 4-NP from environmental water is an urgent and important topic. Currently, the common pathways to remove 4-NP include adsorption, photocatalytic reduction, electrolytic reduction, etc. [4,5] The photocatalytic reduction method, representing the conversion of 4-NP to low-toxic p-aminophenol (4-AP), has aroused significant attention owing to the simplicity, low cost, high conversion rate, etc. [6–9]. Thus, the synthesis and development of photocatalysts with better performance has been an urgent research topic.

At present, the traditional approaches for synthesizing 4-AP include the reduction of nitrobenzene and 4-NP, and the reduction of 4-NP can be further divided into iron powder reduction and hydrogenation reduction methods [10,11]. For these methods, the expensive precious metal-based catalysts are necessary, including Al, Fe, Zn, Pd, Pt, Au, Ag and Cu, leading to the significant enhancement of cost, much more pollution, difficult treatment and recyclability of the catalysts [6,12–14]. Moreover, the harsh reaction conditions such as acid medium also enhance the requirement of the reaction equipment and cause severe corrosion. Therefore, exploring a novel and environmental-friendly approach in the absence of expensive precious metal-based catalysts for the reduction of 4-NP is a promising research orientation. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), as a strong reducing agent, has a positive impact on the reduction of 4-NP, but shows weak reduction of 4-NP without the addition of a catalyst [15]. The reduction rate of 4-NP can be significantly accelerated via introducing a catalyst, which is mainly composed of noble metal or transition metal elements. The noble metal-based catalysts possess high catalytic activity, but are also expensive, difficult to prepare and to recover, which greatly restrict the further development [16–18]. Hence the supported catalysts containing transition metal elements and carrier materials with low cost and convenient synthetic route were developed [19,20]. The carrier materials commonly used by researchers so far include various carbon materials, metal oxides, metal salts, solid super acids, anion and cation exchange resins and other substances [21–24]. The carbon dots (CDs), are novel zero-dimensional carbon materials with particle sizes <10 nm, and with the advantages including non-toxicity, easy preparation, low cost, large-scale synthesis, etc. [25–27] The CDs also possess excellent thermostability, chemical-stability, biocompatibility, and can serve as electron-donor and acceptor, thus exhibiting great prospects in photocatalysis, fluorescence detection, cell image, photovoltaic devices, etc. [28–30] Generally, the introduction of CDs can promote the electron transport of the supported catalysts, further enhancing the photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP.

Herein, the supported catalyst containing transition metal element-based substances Bi2O3/Bi2S3 (BB) nanocomposites and CDs, with facile and convenient synthetic route were successfully prepared in this work and applied in the efficient photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP. The influence of feeding ratio of CDs and BB on the reduction performance was investigated and the 8% of CDs was the best feeding ratio (abbreviated as BB/CDs-8). High-concentrated 4-NP (500 ppm) could be reduced by BB/CDs-8 within 2 min in the presence of NaBH4, and the saturated 4-NP solution can be almost completely reduced within 1 h, showing great potential for industrial application. Moreover, the mechanism of the photocatalytic reduction performance of BB/CDs-8 was also studied and speculated.

L-Aspartic acid (> 99.0%), NaOH (> 99.0%), thiourea (99.0%) were purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co. Ltd.; Bi(NO3)·5H2O (99.0%), DMF (99.5%), ethanol (99.7%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin biochemical Co. Ltd.; HNO3 (98.0%), NaBH4 (> 98.0%) were purchased from the department of lab construction & administration in Changzhou University. All Chemicals were directly used without further purification.

5 g L-Aspartic acid was pyrolyzed in a muffle furnace under air atmosphere at 320 °C for 100 h to produce dark brown powders, which was then dissolved in 50 mL H2O and 2.5 mL NaOH (5 M) solution to obtain dark brown solution. The solution was passed through a syringe micro-filter (0.22 μm) to remove insoluble particles and then the product was isolated as a brown solid via freeze-drying.

0.24 g of Bi(NO3)·5H2O was dissolved in 15 mL DMF, then 0.2 mL HNO3 was added 0.12 g of thiourea was dissolved in 5 mL deionized water and then dropwise added into the above solution with vigorous stirring for 1 h. Afterwards, the mixed solution was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated to 100 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was natural cooled to room temperature, and the product was washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, then dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h.

A series of Bi2O3/Bi2S3/CDs-X (abbreviated as BB/CDs-X) were synthesized with different feeding ratios (X represents the mass ratios of CDs, X = 3%, 5%, 8%, 10%) through stirring for 24 h using ethanol as the solvent. The products ware obtained via rotary evaporation of ethanol and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 30 mg of BB/CDs-X and 4-NP solution (50 mL) were put into a photoreactor vial with a total volume of 50 mL. Under constant stirring, a 300 W white light-emitting diode (LED) was used as the visible light source (λ > 400 nm).

Before visible-light irradiation, 2 mL of NaBH4 aqueous solution (1 M) was added to the above solution. During the photocatalytic reaction, 1 mL of the solution was taken out at a specific interval and then passed through a 0.22 μm micro-filter for the subsequent UV–vis spectroscopy analysis.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) of BB/CDs-X were performed on an X' Pert-Pro MPD. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of samples were obtained on Hitachi S-4700 and Tecnai G20, respectively. The optical properties of the samples were analyzed using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV-3600) and Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Invia, UK, excitation wavelength = 532 nm). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on an ESCALAB MK II with Al-Ka radiation. The electrochemical impedance spectra were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E).

The BB/CDs-X composites were prepared through a facile synthetic route with high yields (Scheme 1 ). The BB and CDs were both synthesized through the solvothermal method and then uniformly mixed through physical blending to obtain BB/CDs-X. Moreover, a series of BB/CDs-X with different feeding ratios of CDs including 3%, 5%, 8% and 10% were also synthesized and the influence of the contents of CDs on the photocatalytic performance were investigated. Obviously, the photoreduction rate of BB/CDs-8 is the highest at the same time periods (Fig. S1a), thus BB/CDs-8 was chosen as the main research object for the further characterization and photocatalytic reduction experiments.

The morphology of the CDs, BB and BB/CDs-8 composites are characterized via SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the size of the petal-like BB is in the range of 1–2 μm, while the sizes of the CDs are approximately 3 nm (Fig. S2). When mixed with CDs, the introduction of CDs does not significantly change the morphology of the BB in the SEM images (Fig. 1b), but the changes are obvious in the TEM images. As shown in Fig. 1 (c-d), the spherical particles of CDs in the range of 3–4 nm are distributed evenly in the petal-like BB, demonstrating the successful synthesis of BB/CDs-8. Fig. 1(e-i) shows the Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope (STEM) images of BB/CDs-8 and the corresponding elemental mapping images including Bi, C, O and S. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrogram exhibits the elemental distribution of the BB/CDs-8 composite, as shown in Fig. 1(f, h, i), the corresponding Bi, O and S elements can be observed and the distributions are consistent with the STEM image. While except for the above area, the EDX spectrum of C element can also be observed on the rest vacancy, demonstrating the successful formation of CDs on the surface of BB.

Fig. 2a shows the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of pure BB, CDs and BB/CDs-8 composites. The powder XRD pattern of CDs shows a broad peak centered at 26.6° owing to the graphitic structures of CDs, which can also be observed in the XRD pattern of BB/CDs-8. The diffraction peaks of BB correspond to the cubic Bi2O3 (JCPDS no.76–2478) and orthorhombic Bi2S3 (JCPDS no.84–0279), respectively. For the composite BB/CDs-8, all the peaks from CDs and BB can be found, indicating the successful formation of BB/CDs-8 composites. As shown in Fig. 2b, the Raman spectrum of BB/CDs-X are consistent with the graphitic content (G-band, 1585 cm−1) and the defects (D-band,1344 cm−1).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed to further explore the chemical state of elements in the BB/CDs-8 composite. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 3a) indicates the surface of BB/CDs-8 contains various elements including Bi, C, N, O and S, which is consistent with the results of STEM elemental mapping. As shown in Fig. 3c, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f mainly consists of four peaks located at 164.0 eV, 158.7 eV (corresponding to BiO bonds) and 163.4 eV, 158.2 eV (corresponding to BiS bonds). The binding energy of BiO bonds and BiS bonds are lower than the values in the previously reported literatures, which is mainly attributed to the interaction between the –COOH groups of the CDs and Bi2O3/Bi2S3 [27,31]. The C 1 s spectrum contains three peaks including 287.6, 285.0, 284.4 eV, which are attributed to the CN, CO, CC bonds, respectively, all originating from the carbon atoms of CDs. The binding energy of S 2 s spectrum is located at 224.7 eV.

The catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP was performed under ambient temperature. With the addition of NaBH4, the color of the solution changed from light yellow to dark yellow due to the variation of pH value. According to the UV–vis spectrum of the reduction process (Fig.S3a), within 1 min, the peak located at 400 nm sharply decreased, while a new peak centered at 300 nm appeared, indicating the successful reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. Within 2 min, the solution is almost transparent, corresponding to the disappearance of the peak located at 400 nm in the UV–vis adsorption spectrum. The reduction reaction process is demonstrated as pseudo-first-order kinetics and the equation is as follows:
K app
= − ln
C
C o

Where K app represents the apparent rate constant, C o and C are the concentration of 4-NP at the initial time and t time, respectively. Consequently, as shown in Fig. S3b, a linear correlation can be established and the K app is calculated as 1.747 min−1, surpassing most of the other catalysts reported in the previous literatures (Table S1).

Fig. 4a shows the photoreduction performance of CDs, BB, BB/CDs-8 in the presence of NaBH4 and BB/CDs-8 without the addition of NaBH4 towards 4-NP (500 ppm) under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). The BB/CDs-8 without the addition of NaBH4 shows almost no photoreduction performance, indicating the essential role of NaBH4 in the catalytic reduction of 4-NP. Moreover, the BB as an electron donor is also a significant factor in the photoreduction process. As shown in Fig. 4b, the catalytic performance of BB/CDs-8 is better compared with BB, realizing complete reduction of 4-NP within 2 min.

To better understand the function of light in the catalytic process, the saturated solution of 4-NP was used to widen the gap of efficiency between light condition and dark condition, and the results are shown in Fig. 4c/d. The influence of light on the catalytic performance of BB towards 4-NP is not obvious, while the light could significantly enhance the photoreduction rate of BB/CDs-8, which can be ascribed to the favorable electric conduction of CDs, demonstrating the importance of CDs in the elevation of light adsorption efficiency.

The EIS Nyquist plots of BB and BB/CDs-8 is displayed in Fig.S4, the arc radius of BB/CDs-8 is much smaller than that of BB, demonstrating the intimate contact between the components, leading to lower resistance and faster interfacial charge transfer. The recyclability and stability experiments were performed and the results are shown in Fig. S5. Slight decrease could be observed in the third cycle owing to the collapse of the structure of BB, which is proved by the change of SEM images in Fig.S6. The unavoidable weight loss of CDs during the process of testing, centrifugation and washing is also a possible factor contributing to the decline of reduction conversion.

The possible reduction process is depicted in Scheme 2 . BH4 − was adsorbed and desorbed simultaneously and reversibly on the surface of BB, and then participated in the reduction of 4-NP, along with the electrons generated by CDs and BB. The NaBH4 is indispensable in the reduction process, which is proved by the photocatalytic reduction performance of sodium tetraphenylboron and the results are shown in Fig.S7. Almost no 4-NP (20 ppm) could be reduced in the presence of sodium tetraphenylboron with the addition of BB/CDs-8 neither in aqueous solution nor in non-protonic solvent, DMSO.

In general, the mechanism of the photocatalytic reduction process of BB/CDs-8 towards 4-NP includes three major steps. Firstly, the BH4 −, as an electron donor, together with the deprotonated 4-NP, are reversibly absorbed on the surface of the BB; Secondly, the electrons derived from the electron donors are transferred to 4-NP, contributing to the efficient reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP; Thirdly, the reduction product 4-AP is spontaneously desorbed from the surface of the BB. Notably, the CDs are also good electron donors and can provide electrons for the electron acceptor, that is, the nitro group of 4-NP. Besides, the CDs are excellent electron conductor and able to rapidly transfer electrons derived from BH4 − and itself to the electron acceptor. Under light irradiation, the CDs can also enhance the light adsorption efficiency of BB/CDs-8, thus boosting the catalytic activities, further improving the photocatalytic reduction performance.

The novel photocatalytic system containing CDs and BB namely BB/CDs-8 was successfully synthesized via a facile and simple approach with high yields. The photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP with high concentration to 4-AP can be rapidly realized with the addition of BB/CDs-8 in the presence of NaBH4. For 4-NP (500 ppm), the solution is almost transparent and the 4-NP is almost completely reduced to 4-AP within 10 min. Moreover, for saturated 4-NP solution, the great majority could be degraded within 1 h, in the presence of light. The photocatalytic reduction mechanism was also depicted and described in detail. Generally, this work can provide a new photocatalytic system for the efficient reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP and potentially applied in the treatment of environmental wastewater containing 4-NP.

J. Jiang, R. Ji, S. Zhou: Synthesis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing - review & editing. P. Wang: Synthesis, Project administration, Software, Data curation – original draft. Z. Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization – review & editing. Q. Xu: Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. P. Gu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Project of Key Laboratory of Organic Synthesis of Jiangsu Province, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University (No. KJS2126). This work was also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803143).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2022.100678.
